speaker: interviewee
text: As I was saying, the dance started. You know? Everything started to-- you know, get impressed in our mind that-- so it is going to be like this, how they will treat us. And, are we like animals?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, these things go through even a young person's mind. You know? And you're wondering what it is, what we have done, and why are they doing this to us? And-- and you overhear the older people talking to each other. And you know that they don't want to talk about everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they started talk about the unthinkable. You know, there were some diabetic people there. And they were saying, what is going to happen when my insulin shot runs out? They-- they couldn't-- I mean, they couldn't-- they could not to disinfect the needles anymore. I mean, even that I understood when I was a kid. You know? I knew about diabetes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were talking about who's going to bury us? What is going to happen? These things I started to hear, you see. And-- and this wasn't so bad because it was still in Hungary, you know, and that we were just going from one place to the other. You know? So it just-- it's for a young kid. You know, I'm talking a way a young kid remembers. And that's what I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and the grownups we're talking. And-- and some grownups we're talking about things that my mother didn't want me to hear. So she kind of put her arm around my ears and so-- so I don't listen to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the children talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, on the train I don't remember any-- any children. I didn't have any friends. I mean, for some reason, we were separated. Everybody was in their own little group, you know, in the cattle car. And I just don't remember. It's the weirdest thing. I don't remember other children. At this time, you know, I did not. But once we got out of the cars--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this, at [? Segwitch? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: At Szeged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Szeged.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Szeged. Once we got out of the car, then we start to form groups with kids. And we started to play hopscotch. You know. Hopscotch is, I think, an international game, isn't it? [LAUGHS] So we started playing that. And then we start playing tag. And then we started playing hide and seek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there rules when you got off the train and arrived at Szeged?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- Szeged. No. There were no rules. They-- there may have been rules for the grownups. But we kids didn't hear about it. So we just went about our business.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, we were playing. And we're having-- having a time, you know. Asking each other, what did you eat? Do you have any candy left? You have anything? What do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you think they're gonna make another study session for us-- things like that. And then we just were running around. And we were constantly getting out of breath. And our mothers were calling us constantly. And in Szeged, we-- I was with my mother. And we're still with the Totkomlos contingent. And my-- you wouldn't believe this, but my mother-- we were walking around this enclosure where they kept us, all the Jews. We were all in the open air. We were all sitting on our valises, our bags, our so on. And my mother and I, we asked somebody to watch our stuff, and we walked around. My mother was always a go-getter and always looking around. She was hoping that she would see her parents, my grandmother and grandfather. And lo and behold, we were walking. And all of a sudden she lets out a big yell, a scream. And she says look, the grandparents are over there. Look, Harry's there. Her-- her brother and sister were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what are her parents' name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was Sam, and my grandmother's name was Malvina. But I've never, naturally, never called her Malvina. I always called her grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- Katz. They were Katz. And my Uncle Harry and Lilly-- I don't know how my mother did it, but she finagled that they would come over from a-- from a different compound. They came over, joined us. And from there on we were together in the Holocaust, the grandparents, the three-- the two sisters and a brother, and myself. It was the most amazing-- amazing thing. I don't know how my mother did it, but this is how it was. And it's all a great story. My uncle Harry, who's here in Toronto, also remembers it. And he still can't believe it, how we-- how we got together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Szeged?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Szeged, we stayed-- excuse me-- we stayed about a day or a day and a half. I don't think it could have been anything more. I don't remember even sleeping there. But I guess we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we saw a lot of people who were sick, and who were-- and I keep thinking about them, even now. They were all so sick. There were about a hundred of them. And I know they didn't survive, not even two weeks. How could they survive? I mean, they were so sick. They all died. Was there any medical attention available for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only medical attention was the doctors who were themselves right then and there. What can they give? They can give them maybe an aspirin if they had any left, or something. they couldn't-- it was just-- just shocking, even to me, you know, as a kid. I remember those things. And-- so we were all together. And-- and I remember that there were transports going all the time, almost every day. There were transports going through Germany and Austria. And there was news that came out that there's-- transport is going to leave tomorrow. And anybody who wants to be on this transport would have to shift and go over to the other side. And my mother said to my grandparents, and Harry, and, Lilly said that we should go. Let's get out of here. It's terrible here. We might as well get where we're going because this is terrible. This is dirty. We can't wash. We can't do-- ugh, everybody was itchy. Everybody was-- can't tell you. I mean, you were still smelling from the-- and we knew that it was this-- this was just a transitory thing. So we wanted to get where we were. And a lot of other people from Totkomlos were telling us, Mrs. Schwartz, don't go. Stay. You-- you'll just-- don't bother. You know, stay and rest up a little bit. You'll have more energy. And you'll-- She says, no. No. We're going. So we went. We went in the next transport. And just as an aside, the people who came after us, the Totkomlos, they didn't survive because they went to Auschwitz. And in Auschwitz-- excuse me-- they killed everybody. I mean, Auschwitz you were gassed. I mean, nobody-- I mean, we didn't know this. But this is what happened afterwards, we found out. So practically the whole town, Totkomlos, was gassed. I mean, everybody, the cantor, the rabbi, the gabbi, the people, the-- the shoemaker, the-- the people who dealt in milk, the-- the dry goods store people, the-- everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that you were saved?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Luck, just luck. I don't know what it is. It's just luck. It was just luck. And, you know, because I spoke a lot about this to my mother, you know, who-- who lived here in Canada, you know, till of '63. And I spoke to her a lot about this. And she says, what luck, she says. What luck, and that we're still together. And at '63, and my grandparents were still alive, and Harry, it was just luck. And everybody else was gassed. There were-- only my mother and I, and maybe somebody else was left in Totkomlos. My mother went back just to claim some of her things. I never went back. She-- she said that the-- the synagogue I loved so much when I was a kid-- it such a beautiful synagogue-- it was totally erased and burnt down. And all the-- the parsonages, everything that we lived there was gone. All the Jewish stores, gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I was-- I was really devastated, I-- you know, when I was a kid. I didn't cry, but I-- I was so sad, you know, to find out, you know, how-- how they could do to us, you know, and what they could do to people. And how they-- why? How could people behave like that towards other people just because they had different religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's very difficult. So in any case, we left Szeged. And everybody else went into their own destiny. You know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go from Szeged?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went from Szeged. We went to-- they were taking us. It's funny. But they took us the southern route. They took us through Vienna to Strasshof. Now, Strasshof was a distribution camp. We did not know what it was beforehand. But afterwards, we found out everything, you know, from the-- from the newspaper articles and people who knew about this, you know, the machinations and everything. We found out that Strasshof was a distribution camp. And we went through Vienna. Again, they took us at right, in the morning, around 1 o'clock, when it was dark, when people didn't know us. They took us in trams, regular streetcars.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought we were going to go somewhere, maybe to a restaurant or something. Or-- although, I didn't know much about restaurants. Maybe I thought we were going to go to a pub where we can get some spritzer or something, you know? Did anybody try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. We didn't even think about it. We just didn't. We didn't. We went like sheep. I mean, I can't believe it. But we-- well, who-- who was going to escape? The older people? Where will they go? First of all, they were too weak. They didn't have any money. They were like so conspicuous, it's unbelievable. We didn't have any young people around.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the young people were conscripted into the workforce. Some of the other young people were there. They were practically hostages with their mothers and sisters there. How can they-- they go? We couldn't go anywhere. We were-- we were-- there was just nothing. We couldn't do. And we weren't violent people. So we didn't say, all right. We're going to shoot our way out. And if we die, we die. But we'll take a couple with them, you know? We didn't even think of such a thing. We just didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's the way it was. So we never-- we went through Vienna. And then we went to another one of these train stations. I'm not exactly sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: It had a name, I'm sure. Vienna has several big train stations. And then they put us into cattle cars. And then that trip was really bad, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went to Strasshof, that was a bad trip. A lot of people died, maybe three or four. And then after a while, you know, we saw so much death that we-- our hearts became hardened. And we became jaded. We-- when somebody died, it didn't matter. They just died. They-- we called them lucky, and they died. And we can't kill ourselves, because first of all, we are chicken to kill ourselves, or we just thought about maybe we'll be saved. Or-- And we children, we didn't think on such lines, again. But there were a lot of people who committed suicide, but-- we-- a lot of intellectuals and rich people committed suicide who couldn't take--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they commit suicide?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just killed themselves with a pill or something. They had some pills hidden away or something. Cyanide-- not cyanide-- arsenic, you know? We had a couple of people who had drugstores. [? Partikcak ?] [? Partaka ?]. And they just killed themselves with these things, and-- not-- not violently with knives or shooting. First of all, we didn't have anything to do that, you know, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: You indicated that this train ride was the worst. What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. This train ride wasn't as bad as the train ride from Strasshof to Belsen. That was really bad. I mean, that train ride was, oh. But this train ride, you know, about three or four diabetics died on our-- in our wagon. It wasn't so bad. It wasn't as tight-- as tightly fitted as they did it in Hungary from Saigon. But it was pretty tight. But-- only three or four people died. And they were mainly diabetics. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived in Strasshof?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived, they-- they separated the men from the women. And they took us immediately for a bath-- showers. OK? They made us all undress. And everybody tried to keep their clothes, you know, in their little clumps so they recognized it. And my mother was afraid for me. She didn't let me go with the men. So she kept me with the women. So I was the only boy with a multitude of naked women. I mean, to a young kid to see all these naked-- I never even saw my mother naked, practically. Never mind all these old, young, skinny, fat, ugly, beautiful. They're all-- and it's-- and every-- everything was so depressing. It wasn't-- it was dreamlike. It was-- they all took us. And then they all washed us like they washed cattles, you know, and things. They didn't hose us or anything. But we stood under the showers. And everybody was so violated. You know, all the women, I could see them. They were so-- and then there were men gazing at them. And they didn't look at them after a lot. Even they were ashamed, the Germans. And they just-- it was-- it was just a humiliating experience for everybody, which is unbelievably humiliating. To me, of course, it's probably left me scarred. It must have left me scarred. I don't know how. But it-- it must left me, because I was a little boy. And-- but I was with my mother, and I survived it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then so as soon as we are finished, they made us get dressed. And then they powdered us with DDT, you know? They put DDT right to our bodies. Of course, now we know DDT is toxic, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in those days, they figured DDT, you know, just got rid of lice. But they powdered us and so on. And-- a-- and we got dressed. And we feeled refreshed, [LAUGHS] if you could call it refreshed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- then they stuffed us into the cattle car. That was unbelievable because it was almost impossible to breathe, we were so tight. And-- and the-- and the windows weren't open. And the-- and the-- the windows, with-- they were covered with these-- with this wires, you know, stinging wires. You know? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I went to the Holocaust Museum in-- in-- in Washington, a-- as you walk through the exhibit, they put you through the cattle car. And the cattle car's window is open. And the light shine through. And I remember going at night. We were all standing. And the window was open, but with the-- you know, with the barbed wire and all across. And-- and the moon shone in. And you know, sometimes when, you know, you're walking, and you hear a bell, you smell something, and all of a sudden it takes you back 20 years because you smell something that you smelled when you were a kid. And all of a sudden you're transported into a beautiful garden or the beautiful experience that you experienced when you were a kid. Here when I was in Washington, when I saw this light streaming in through the cattle window, you could see the-- the faces looking up through the window, and then the moon shining at them. And you could see their profiles, and the horrible looks, you know, about-- you know, gasping for breath.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it brought me back in Washington. It was one of my-- I nearly fainted, you know, when I got into that cattle car. And my wife was asking, what? So I told her what's happening to me, you know, what-- what is bringing back this light and just feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when we went further in the exhibit, we saw-- they listed the town that were totally destroyed and erased by the populace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me take you back, through, to this actual trip on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you feeling then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it-- it was very-- very uncomfortable. But with me, it was OK because my mother shielded me. She had me in her arms all the time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were people talking about on this transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were yelling at each other, you know, move yourself. They were talking about when Mashiach will come, when the Messiah will come. They were talking about garbled things. You know, you couldn't talk about anything. They said, when are they going to let us out? I can't breathe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people die on this transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, yeah. This-- we had about 10, 12 corpses. Ah, we pushed them all down, so it gave us more room. They were all down. We were tramping on them. But thank god, some people died. Because we were all going to die. We couldn't breathe on this thing. They stuck us in so-- so closely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the corpses?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they were just taken out. When we got to Belsen, we all got out. And then the corpse-- corpses were later taken out. And they were driven away. They were probably burnt like the other corpses. They were burnt in Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived in Belsen after all this-- it was just a horrible trip. I mean, people went mad on these trips. Some people were just talking to themselves constantly. You know, this-- you know when somebody you see on the street that they prattle to themselves all the time. And there's-- they go--

speaker: interviewee
text: When two or three people do this on a cattle car that is full of people, and they are suffering, it's just-- it's like out of hell. It's like bedlam. It's unbelievable. [SIGHS] It's-- it's-- Well, anyway, so we finally arrived to Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing that you saw when they opened the doors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was actually in Belsen. It was a beautiful day. It was a crisp day with the sun shining. It was-- we just saw a beautiful sight. We-- I think they let us out I think five miles out of town, or maybe 12 kilometers. Then we had to walk the rest of the way to Belsen. It was a beautiful day. It was a stunning day. And that's what we saw. We saw just gorgeous. The birds were chirping. And-- I don't know, it was weird. They made us, you know, stand up. They counted us. I did something wrong. And that's when one of the officers, you know, with his rod beat me on the back a couple of times because I got out of line.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were other people beaten as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't hurt. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were other people beaten as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We heard screams, and yells, and so on. But I-- I didn't see anybody myself being beaten. I remember with kids we're always yakking, and we're talking to each other. And then we walked all the way into Belsen. And then, that's when the first time we saw the [? hefflings, ?] you the people, the men with the striped outfits, you know, the very skinny guys. I mean they were skinny. And they were working. We couldn't see how they survived. And then people were begging us for food. There were other people already in there, you know, a longer time. And as we were walking towards our compound, they were begging us for food. So we-- we tossed them food that we had, that we still had left, because-- And then a lot of people from there told us, don't toss your food away. You'll be needing it, because you'll be dying like we are dying. Then there were all the people piled, you know, like corpses. They were about-- about 15 on the bottom, and then went up as a pyramid. And they said, see, these people died because they smoked. But-- ironic. They didn't die because they smoked. They died because they exchanged their food for cigarettes. And they died of starvation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why they died because of smoking, not because the smoking killed them. But a lot of people died like this because they were big smokers. So they-- You were still young boy. What did you think? What did you feel when you saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought it was a horrible adventure. I was-- by this time, I was already 7. I was practically 8. Yeah. I was already 8. Oh, I forgot to tell you. That-- I have to backtrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because our first tip from Strasshof was to Floitensberg. I forgot. That was to our work camp where we worked. It was when we went into our village. OK? So Strasshof comes-- I mean, Belsen comes after Strasshof. So first we went to Floitensberg. So just call this as a digression. But-- so anyway, what we were thinking as kids? What could we think? It's hard to tell you what we could think when we were kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just in a certain stage in Belsen when we started feeling that perhaps we're going to die. Really, we're going to die. But before Belsen, we went to Floitensberg. Then we were there for about six months on our work detail, you know, where we worked. I mean, we helped the farmers in their work. Because a lot of the men, they were taken away to the Russian front, a lot of the German men. So they only had women left. So we were the ones that were helping them to bring in the harvest, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, we stayed in barns in-- in that place. And there was the rabbi from Szarvas in that place. The rabbi and his sons were there in Floitensberg. And all of us, we were in-- in this barn, which was actually quite pleasant. We had food. I made friends with some horses, you know, those big Clydesdales. Was the rabbi treated differently--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --than the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He was just the same. He was working just as hard. Exactly the same, there was no difference. There was no-- no. There was nothing. Everybody was treated equally badly. And-- so I remember this horse. And then the men who took care of this course was a Finnish guy from Finland. And they used to eat their bread. They had butter, you know, this thick. It was unbelievable. No wonder the Finish guys died of heart attacks. But they had butter bread. Of course, I had some of the butter bread too. Made friends with some dogs. You know what kids do. You know, they run around. And I remember lots of cherry trees. You know, and they're beautiful, big cherries. They were this big. And we had a stomach ache constantly because we kids were eating cherries. And then from there, from Floitensberg, from that place, that's when we went to Belsen. And that was the-- well, from Floitensberg, we went back to Strasshof again, the distribution camp, where they gave us a shower again. And then they took us to Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- that was around '44 or '45, in that-- in that time when they took us to Belsen. And I still remember from Floitensberg was the first time we started really getting close to hear the battle, OK? The battle for-- from-- there was a city near us, from Floitensberg. They were bombarding it almost every night. We could see the flashes. And we could see the dull thuds all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we knew that, you know, every time we saw the planes going overhead, I started shuddering, because here there were all way up high. And they just went [HUMMING SOUND], the drones of the planes. Even now, when I hear a plane droning like that, you know, I get shivers down my back because I know they're bombing. And-- so back to Belsen again, so after we went from Floitensberg back to Strasshof, and then from Strasshof to Belsen, that was the real bad trip and the final trip to Belsen. And-- and as I already explained, it was a beautiful day, and so on. And we walked into the place. And then they assigned the barrack for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the number of your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I do not remember. I know that they kept the families together. They were called [? Zomdilager. ?] After the war, we found out they kept us. There was a purpose. OK? They-- they-- they were doing experiments with a lot of people, with a lot of families. They kept the families together. And the families, naturally, the Germans being very-- very organized, they started with the A's, with the people whose names started with A's, and then the B's, and the C's. Slowly, we saw people disappearing. But we didn't know where they went, OK, or what they did. So it just-- it just went on and on until-- until the English started bombarding, OK, Bergen-Belsen. But funny thing is, you know, in Belsen, you know, we kids, we used to play around. And to us, we were so cool towards death and corpses. To us, it didn't even matter. We used to play close to huge indentation where they sent the corpses. And we thought they were pigs because they-- some of them were still moving. And then we got closer, we realized that those corpses were moving. [SIGHS]
